reasoning: Mahmoud Khalil, a lawful permanent resident, was ruled "removable" by an immigration judge on April 11, 2025. However, this ruling only initiated the next phase of his legal defense. His attorneys had until April 23 to file applications for relief from deportation, including asylum, which requires further immigration court hearings. If denied, he has the right to appeal to the Board of Immigration Appeals (BIA), which typically takes months to years. In parallel, a federal district judge in New Jersey (Judge Michael Farbiarz) is overseeing Khalil’s habeas corpus lawsuit and has expressly prohibited the government from deporting him while those proceedings are active. On April 29, Judge Farbiarz ruled that Khalil's federal lawsuit can proceed, and on May 7, he demanded the government detail its legal grounds for his deportation. Given the multiple layers of pending appeals, mandatory hearings for his relief claims, and an active federal stay on his deportation, it is highly improbable that the legal process will be exhausted and he will be physically deported before July 1, 2025 (less than two months away from the May 14 cutoff). Legal experts estimate the process will take 1-2 years even if he remains detained.
